Psychology of Motivation
 Comprehensive Study Notes
Contents
-> Q1 — Nature and Properties of Motivation
-> Q2 — Conceptual Issues of Motivation in Understanding Human Behaviour
-> Q3 — Contribution of Lorenz to the Understanding of Motivation
-> Q4 — Historical Development of Motivational Concepts
-> Q5 — Response Dimensions of Motivation and Their Utility in Research
-> Q6 — History and Current Status of Motivation in Psychology

Question
1
Nature and Properties of Motivation
Introduction
Motivation is a complex psychological concept. Its nature can be understood by examining its key
characteristics and the way it functions within human behavior. Psychologists have described the nature of
motivation through several important points. Understanding both the nature and properties of motivation
helps us explain why people behave the way they do in different situations. Part A: Nature of Motivation
1. Motivation is an Internal Process
Motivation originates from within the individual. It is an internal psychological state that energizes and
directs behavior toward a goal. It cannot always be seen directly but is inferred from behavior. Example: A person feels an inner urge to eat when hungry. This drive comes from inside the body, not from
outside. 2. Motivation is Dynamic
Motivation is not fixed or static. It keeps changing with time, experience, age, and circumstances. What
motivates a person today may not motivate them tomorrow. Human needs and desires are always
evolving. Example: A child is motivated by toys and games; an adult is motivated by career goals and financial
security. 3. Motivation is Goal-Directed
All motivated behavior is aimed at achieving a specific goal or satisfying a need. Without a goal, behavior
has no direction. Goals give motivation its focus and purpose. Example: A student works hard throughout the year with the goal of passing with first division. 4.

Motivation Involves Biological and Psychological Factors
Motivation has both biological roots (hunger, thirst, sex) and psychological roots (achievement,
recognition, self-esteem). Both aspects work together to guide behavior. Example: A person eats to satisfy hunger (biological) but may also eat at a restaurant to enjoy a social
experience (psychological). 5. Motivation is Universal
All living beings are motivated. Every organism has needs that must be satisfied for survival and
well-being. The specific motives may differ, but motivation itself is present in all creatures.

Example: Just as animals search for food when hungry, humans seek food, shelter, relationships, and
achievement. 6. Motivation May Be Conscious or Unconscious
Sometimes people are fully aware of their motives (conscious motivation). At other times, behavior is
driven by hidden, unconscious desires or conflicts that the person may not recognize. Example: A person may unconsciously seek approval from others due to deep-rooted feelings of insecurity. 7. Motivation Influences Emotions and Cognition
Motivation is closely linked to emotions and thinking. A motivated person feels enthusiasm and positive
emotions; blocked motivation leads to frustration and negative emotions. It also sharpens attention and
memory. Example: A student who is motivated to learn feels excited and concentrates better during study. 8. Motivation is Cyclical
Motivation follows a cycle: a need arises, it creates a drive, behavior is directed toward a goal, the goal is
achieved, the need is satisfied, and a new need arises. This cycle continues throughout life. Example: Hunger arises, person searches for food, food is eaten, hunger is satisfied, and after some time
hunger returns again. Part B: Properties (Characteristics) of Motivation
Motivation is the internal process that activates, guides, and maintains behavior toward achieving a goal. It
gives energy and direction to human behavior and helps people fulfill their needs, desires, and ambitions.

Psychologists study motivation to understand why people behave in certain ways. Example: A student studies hard to get good marks, and a hungry person searches for food. Both behaviors
are motivated by needs and goals. 1. Motivation is Goal-Oriented
Motivation always directs behavior toward a particular goal or objective. People act because they want to
achieve something. Goals provide direction and purpose to behavior. Example: A student studies daily to secure first division in examinations. 2. Motivation is an Internal State
Motivation begins within the individual. It comes from inner needs, desires, emotions, or drives. Internal
conditions such as hunger, thirst, curiosity, or achievement needs create motivation. Example: A thirsty person feels an inner urge to drink water. 3. Motivation Energizes Behavior
Motivation increases energy and effort in performing activities. It pushes a person to take action. Without
motivation, behavior becomes weak and inactive.

Example: An athlete practices for many hours before a competition. 4. Motivation is Continuous
Human needs never completely end. After one need is satisfied, another need appears. Therefore,
motivation is a continuous process throughout life. Example: After getting a job, a person may work hard for promotion and later for higher salary. 5. Motivation can be Positive or Negative
Positive motivation: behavior is directed toward gaining rewards. Negative motivation: behavior occurs to
avoid punishment or failure. Both types influence human actions. Example: Positive: A child studies to receive praise from parents. Negative: A worker completes work on
time to avoid criticism from the boss. 6. Motivation Varies from Person to Person
Different individuals are motivated by different needs and interests. Personality, experiences, culture, and
environment all influence the nature and direction of motivation. Example: One student studies for knowledge, while another studies only for marks. 7. Motivation can be Intrinsic or Extrinsic
Intrinsic motivation: a person performs an activity for personal satisfaction or interest. Extrinsic motivation:
a person performs an activity to gain external rewards or avoid punishment. Example: Intrinsic: A child paints pictures because he enjoys painting. Extrinsic: An employee works hard for
salary or promotion. 8. Motivation Affects Performance
Higher motivation generally improves performance and efficiency.

Motivation increases concentration,
persistence, and effort, leading to better outcomes. Example: A motivated employee completes tasks more effectively than an uninterested employee. 9. Motivation Helps in Need Satisfaction
Motivation arises because of unsatisfied needs and continues until the need is fulfilled. According to drive
reduction theory, needs create tension, and motivation reduces that tension. Example: Hunger motivates a person to search for food. 10. Motivation Influences Learning and Behavior
Motivation plays an important role in learning, memory, and achievement. Interest and desire increase
attention and active participation in learning. Example: Students who are motivated learn faster and remember better. Types of Needs Related to Motivation

Psychologists explain that motivation is connected with different kinds of needs:
Biological needs: hunger, thirst, sleep
Social needs: love, friendship, approval
Psychological needs: achievement, power, self-esteem
Example: A person joins social groups to fulfill the need for belongingness. Importance of Motivation
Motivation is important because it:
Encourages goal achievement
Improves learning and performance
Increases confidence and persistence
Helps in personality development
Creates enthusiasm and interest in work
Conclusion
Motivation is an essential part of human behavior. By nature, it is internal, dynamic, goal-directed,
cyclical, and influenced by both biological and psychological factors. Its properties show that it can be
positive or negative, intrinsic or extrinsic, and that it varies from person to person. It gives direction,
energy, and purpose to actions. Understanding the nature and properties of motivation helps
psychologists explain learning, performance, emotions, and human behavior in daily life.

Question
2
Conceptual Issues of Motivation in Understanding
Human Behaviour
Introduction
Motivation is the process that initiates, directs, and maintains human behavior toward goals. Psychologists
study motivation to understand why people think, feel, and behave in particular ways. However, motivation
is a complex concept because human behavior is influenced by biological, psychological, social, and
environmental factors. Different psychologists have raised several conceptual issues regarding motivation
that help in understanding the true nature of human behavior. 1. Nature versus Nurture Issue
Whether motivation is caused by biological factors (nature) or environmental factors (nurture). Some
motivations are innate such as hunger and thirst; many are learned through experience, culture, and social
interaction. Example: A child may naturally feel hunger, but food preferences are learned from family and culture. Psychological Understanding: Human behavior is influenced by both heredity and environment. 2. Conscious versus Unconscious Motivation
Whether people are fully aware of their motives. Conscious motivation: people knowingly work toward
goals. Unconscious motivation: behavior is influenced by hidden desires or conflicts the person is unaware
of. Example: A person may become overly competitive because of unconscious feelings of insecurity. Psychological Understanding: According to Sigmund Freud, unconscious motives strongly influence
behavior. 3.

Internal versus External Motivation
Behavior may arise from internal needs (intrinsic motivation for personal satisfaction) or external rewards
(extrinsic motivation guided by rewards or punishments). Example: A person reads books because he enjoys learning (intrinsic); an employee works hard for salary
(extrinsic). Psychological Understanding: Both intrinsic and extrinsic factors affect human behavior. 4. Need Reduction versus Growth Motivation
Some psychologists believe motivation aims to reduce tension; others believe humans seek growth and
self-development beyond basic need satisfaction. Example: An artist paints not for survival but for self-expression and creative fulfillment. Psychological Understanding: Abraham Maslow explained that humans move from basic needs to
higher psychological needs.

5. Hedonistic versus Achievement Motivation
Whether people only seek pleasure and avoid pain, or also work hard for achievement, excellence, and
long-term goals. Example: A sportsperson practices daily to win a medal despite the pain and effort involved. Psychological Understanding: David McClelland emphasized the need for achievement as an important
human motive. 6. Stability versus Change in Motivation
Some motives remain stable throughout life (need for food and safety), while others change according to
situations and life stages. Example: A teenager may seek friendship, while an adult may focus on career success. Psychological Understanding: Human motivation is dynamic and flexible. 7. Individual Differences in Motivation
Different people may behave differently even in the same situation due to their personality, interests,
values, and life experiences. Example: Two students in the same class: one studies for knowledge, another studies only for marks. Psychological Understanding: Personality, interests, values, and experiences all influence motivation. 8. Conflict of Motives
A person may experience conflicting motives simultaneously: Approach-Approach (two attractive goals),
Avoidance-Avoidance (two unpleasant situations), or Approach-Avoidance (same goal has both positive
and negative aspects). Example: A person wants a high-paying job but dislikes the stress involved. Psychological Understanding: Conflicts create tension and affect decision-making behavior. 9.

Role of Social and Cultural Factors
Human motivation is greatly influenced by society and culture. In some cultures success and competition
are highly valued; in others cooperation and group harmony are more important. Example: Social approval, family expectations, and cultural values shape individual motivation. Psychological Understanding: Social, cultural, and environmental contexts are essential to
understanding motivation. Importance of Studying These Issues
Explain complex human behavior
Understand personality differences
Improve learning and performance
Help in counseling and therapy
Understand emotional and social adjustment

Conclusion
Motivation is a complex process influenced by biological, psychological, social, and cultural factors. The
conceptual issues of motivation help psychologists understand why human behavior differs from person
to person and situation to situation. Motivation may be conscious or unconscious, internal or external,
stable or changing. Therefore, understanding motivation is essential for explaining human behavior in
everyday life.

Question
3
Contribution of Lorenz to the Understanding of
Motivation
Introduction
Konrad Lorenz was an Austrian zoologist and psychologist who made important contributions to the study
of motivation and animal behavior. He is known as one of the founders of ethology, the scientific study of
animal behavior in natural environments. Lorenz explained motivation mainly through the concept of
instinctive behavior, arguing that many behaviors are inborn and biologically determined. 1. Instinct Theory of Motivation
Lorenz argued that many human and animal behaviors are controlled by inherited instincts. Behavior is
biologically programmed and inherited, not learned through experience. Example: A newborn baby automatically sucks milk from the mother — an instinctive behavior requiring no
learning. Importance: Lorenz showed that motivation is not always learned; some motives are innate and
biologically fixed. 2. Hydraulic Model of Motivation
Lorenz proposed the Hydraulic Model to explain how instinctive energy builds up inside the organism. Energy accumulates inside the body; when it becomes strong enough, behavior is released. External
stimuli can also trigger this release. Just like water pressure builds inside a pipe until it overflows,
motivational energy builds inside the organism until action occurs. Example: A hungry lion searches for prey as hunger energy increases to a critical level.

Importance: The model explains the internal buildup of drive states and how they lead to action. 3. Innate Releasing Mechanism (IRM)
Lorenz introduced the concept that certain environmental stimuli automatically trigger instinctive behavior
without any learning. These stimuli are called sign stimuli, and the internal mechanism they activate is the
IRM. Example: A mother bird immediately feeds her chicks when she hears their chirping sound. Importance: Lorenz explained how specific external signals activate internal motivation and release fixed
behavior. 4. Fixed Action Patterns (FAP)
Instinctive behaviors occur in fixed, predictable sequences called Fixed Action Patterns. These are inborn,
similar in all members of a species, automatic, stereotyped, and cannot be stopped once they have begun. Example: A spider spinning a web or birds migrating south during winter. Importance: Some motivated behaviors are universal and biologically controlled across an entire species.

5. Imprinting
Imprinting is a rapid form of learning occurring early in life where young animals become attached to the
first moving object they see. It occurs during a critical period and has a lasting, irreversible effect on future
behavior. Example: Lorenz found that baby geese hatched in his presence followed him everywhere as if he were their
mother. Importance: Early experiences strongly influence motivation and attachment; some learning occurs only
during specific critical periods. 6. Aggression as an Instinct
Lorenz studied aggression extensively and believed it was an innate instinct important for survival. He
argued that aggression has biological roots and serves adaptive purposes such as territory defense and
competition for mates. Example: Animals fight to protect territory or defend their young ones. Importance: Aggression has a biological basis and is not purely a learned behavior.

Evaluation of Lorenz's Contribution
Merits
Scientific study of natural behavior in realistic field settings
Emphasized the role of heredity and instincts in motivation
Contributed to the development of modern ethology and behavioral science
Imprinting studies improved understanding of bonding, attachment, and early development
Criticisms
Overemphasis on instincts — ignored learning and environmental influences
Limited application to complex human behavior which is far more flexible
Hydraulic Model is not fully scientific; motivational energy cannot be easily measured
Conclusion
Konrad Lorenz made major contributions to the understanding of motivation through his studies of
instincts, imprinting, aggression, and animal behavior. He explained that many motivated behaviors are
innate and biologically based. Although his theory has some limitations, his work greatly influenced
modern psychology including developmental, evolutionary, and comparative psychology, and helped in
understanding both animal and human behavior.

Question
4
Historical Development of Motivational Concepts
Introduction
Motivation refers to the internal and external forces that energize, direct, and maintain behavior. The study
of motivation has developed gradually over time, moving from simple instinct theories to modern cognitive
and humanistic explanations of behavior. 1. Ancient Philosophical Views (Pre-Scientific Era)
Philosophers like Plato and Aristotle believed human behavior is guided by reason, desire, and willpower. People act to achieve pleasure and avoid pain. These ideas formed the foundation for later psychological
theories of motivation. Example: A person controls anger through reasoning and self-discipline. 2. Instinct Theory (Late 19th - Early 20th Century)
Psychologists like William McDougall, Sigmund Freud, and Konrad Lorenz explained behavior through
inborn, automatic instincts necessary for survival. Emphasized biological factors in motivation. Criticism:
too many instincts proposed; cannot explain all learned human behavior. Example: Birds building nests without any prior learning. 3. Drive Reduction Theory (1940s) — Clark Hull
Biological needs create tension or drives; behavior occurs to reduce the tension and restore balance. Formula: Need leads to Drive which leads to Behavior which leads to Need Satisfaction. Criticism: could
not explain curiosity, creativity, or adventure-seeking. Example: Hunger motivates eating; thirst motivates drinking. 4.

Psychoanalytic View — Sigmund Freud
Motivation is driven by unconscious instincts including the Life instinct (Eros) and Death instinct
(Thanatos). Unconscious desires and conflicts are powerful motivators. Criticism: difficult to test
scientifically; overemphasized sexual drives. Example: A person behaves aggressively because of hidden unconscious conflicts. 5. Behaviorist Approach — Watson and Skinner
Behavior is motivated by rewards and punishments from the environment. Focus on observable behavior
only; internal states were ignored. Scientific and objective approach. Criticism: ignored thoughts, feelings,
and internal motives. Example: A child studies hard to receive praise from parents. 6. Humanistic Theory — Abraham Maslow

Humans are growth-oriented and move through a hierarchy of needs: Physiological, Safety, Love and
Belongingness, Esteem, and Self-Actualization. Emphasized personal growth and reaching one's full
potential. Criticism: needs may not always follow a fixed order. Example: After fulfilling food and safety needs, a person seeks respect and personal growth. 7. Cognitive Theories — Tolman and Bandura
People are motivated by beliefs, expectations, goals, and decision-making processes. Behavior is
purposeful and directed by mental processes. Criticism: sometimes ignores emotional and biological
factors. Example: A student studies because he expects success and believes in his own ability. 8. Achievement Motivation — David McClelland
Three important human needs: Need for Achievement (nAch), Need for Power (nPow), and Need for
Affiliation (nAff). These needs explain differences in work motivation and leadership behavior. Example: An entrepreneur works hard to achieve business success and gain recognition. 9. Modern Biological and Social Approaches
Modern psychologists combine biological (brain mechanisms, hormones, neurotransmitters),
psychological, and social and cultural factors for a comprehensive understanding of motivation. Example: Stress, emotions, and peer pressure all influence motivation in daily life. Conclusion
The concept of motivation has developed from philosophical ideas and instinct theories to modern
cognitive and humanistic explanations.

Early psychologists focused on instincts and biological drives,
while later theories emphasized learning, unconscious processes, personal growth, and cognitive
factors. Today, motivation is understood as a complex interaction of biological, psychological, and social
influences that guide human behavior.

Question
5
Response Dimensions of Motivation and Their
Utility in Motivational Research
Introduction
Motivation is reflected through different types of responses shown by individuals during behavior. Psychologists study these responses to understand the strength, direction, and nature of motivation. These responses are called response dimensions of motivation, and they help researchers measure
motivation scientifically and understand human behavior more accurately. 1. Direction of Behavior
The choice of a particular goal or activity among many alternatives. Motivation guides behavior toward
specific goals and away from others. Example: A student chooses to study psychology instead of history due to personal interest. Utility in Research: Helps understand preferences, interests, goal choices, and decision-making
behavior. Useful in career counseling and educational guidance. 2. Intensity of Behavior
The amount of effort or energy a person puts into an activity. Greater motivation usually produces stronger
and more sustained effort. Example: Two students study for the same exam, but one studies much harder and for longer hours. Utility in Research: Helps measure strength of motivation, work performance, and competitive behavior. Researchers observe speed, effort, and persistence. 3. Persistence or Duration
How long a person continues behavior despite difficulties or obstacles. Highly motivated individuals do not
give up easily and keep working toward their goal.

Example: An athlete continues daily practice even after repeated failures and injuries. Utility in Research: Helps study achievement motivation, frustration tolerance, and long-term
goal-directed behavior. Useful in educational and organizational psychology. 4. Frequency of Response
How often a motivated behavior occurs within a given time period. Frequent behavior may indicate strong
and recurring motivation. Example: A child repeatedly asks for food when hungry. Utility in Research: Researchers use frequency to measure habits, study learning patterns, and analyze
repetitive behaviors. Useful in behavior modification studies. 5. Latency of Response

The time taken to respond after a stimulus appears. Strong motivation generally reduces response time,
making reactions faster and more immediate. Example: A hungry person immediately reacts when food is offered. Utility in Research: Useful in experimental psychology, learning research, and studies of attention and
reaction time. 6. Physiological Responses
Motivation produces bodily changes such as increased heartbeat, sweating, salivation, and hormonal
changes. The body becomes physiologically activated during strong motivational states. Example: An anxious student before an examination shows increased heartbeat and sweating. Utility in Research: Helps study emotional motivation, stress and anxiety, and the biological basis of
behavior using scientific instruments. 7. Emotional Responses
Motivation is often accompanied by emotions. Emotions reflect the satisfaction or blockage of motives and
provide important information about the person's motivational state. Example: Success creates happiness and pride; failure causes frustration and disappointment. Utility in Research: Researchers study emotional responses to understand human adjustment,
frustration, conflict, and emotional intelligence. 8. Quality of Performance
The standard and quality of behavior produced. High motivation improves concentration, care, and
involvement, leading to higher quality output. Example: A motivated employee performs work more carefully and efficiently than an unmotivated one.

Utility in Research: Researchers examine productivity, academic achievement, and skill development. Important in industrial and educational psychology. Methods Used to Study Response Dimensions
Observation method
Experimental method
Interviews and questionnaires
Physiological recording devices
Performance tests
Criticism
Human motivation is complex and cannot be measured fully through observable responses alone. The
same behavior may arise from entirely different motives in different individuals. Example: A student may study either from genuine interest or from fear of failure — same behavior, different
motivation.

Conclusion
Response dimensions of motivation — direction, intensity, persistence, frequency, latency, physiological
responses, emotional reactions, and quality of performance — are important indicators of human
behavior. They help psychologists understand motivation scientifically and are highly useful in
motivational research, education, counseling, industry, and clinical psychology, providing valuable
insight into why people behave differently in different situations.

Question
6
History and Current Status of Motivation in the
Field of Psychology
Introduction
Motivation is one of the most important concepts in psychology. It explains why individuals start, continue,
and stop certain behaviors. The study of motivation helps psychologists understand human needs,
desires, emotions, learning, and goal-directed behavior. The concept has developed gradually from
philosophical roots to modern scientific approaches, and today it is studied through multiple perspectives. Historical Development
1. Philosophical Period
Plato and Aristotle argued that human behavior is guided by reason, desire, and will. People act to achieve
pleasure and avoid pain. These ideas provided the foundation for later theories. Evaluation: Provided the foundation for later theories but lacked scientific evidence and experimental
support. 2. Instinct Theory — McDougall, Freud, Lorenz
Behavior is driven by inherited instincts necessary for survival. Biological factors were highlighted as
primary motivators. Merits: Highlighted biological factors. Criticism: Too many instincts were proposed; cannot explain learned
behavior. 3. Drive Reduction Theory — Clark Hull
Unsatisfied biological needs create tension; behavior reduces this tension and restores biological balance. Merits: Scientific and measurable explanation. Criticism: Cannot explain curiosity, exploration, or creativity. 4.

Psychoanalytic Approach — Freud
Behavior is influenced by unconscious desires, sexual and aggressive instincts that the person is not
aware of. Merits: Emphasized unconscious motivation. Criticism: Difficult to test scientifically; overemphasized
sexuality. 5. Behaviorist Approach — Watson and Skinner
Behavior is motivated by rewards and punishments from the environment. Focus entirely on observable,
measurable behavior. Merits: Scientific and objective approach. Criticism: Ignored thoughts, feelings, and internal mental states.

6. Humanistic Theory — Maslow
Humans move through a hierarchy of needs: Physiological, Safety, Love and Belongingness, Esteem, and
Self-Actualization. Emphasized positive human growth. Merits: Positive view of human nature; emphasized self-development. Criticism: Needs may not follow a
fixed order. 7. Cognitive Theories — Tolman and Bandura
Behavior is influenced by expectations, goals, beliefs, and decision-making. Mental processes are central
to understanding motivation. Merits: Explains purposeful goal-directed behavior. Criticism: Sometimes ignores emotional and biological
factors. Current Status of Motivation in Psychology
Today, motivation is viewed as a complex multidimensional process involving biological, psychological,
social, and cultural factors working together. 1. Biological Approach
Studies brain mechanisms, hormones, and neurotransmitters. Research shows dopamine is closely
related to reward, pleasure, and motivated behavior. 2. Cognitive and Social Approaches
Study self-efficacy, goal-setting theory, and social influence on motivation. Students are motivated when
they believe in their own ability to succeed. 3. Achievement Motivation — McClelland
The three needs — achievement, power, and affiliation — continue to be widely used to explain work
motivation, leadership behavior, and entrepreneurship. 4.

Self-Determination Theory
Modern theory explaining motivation through autonomy (freedom to choose), competence (ability to
succeed), and relatedness (connection with others). Employees work better when all three are satisfied. Critical Evaluation
Strengths of Modern Motivation Research
Use of scientific methods including experiments, brain imaging, and questionnaires
Multidimensional approach combining biology, cognition, emotion, and environment
Practical applications in education, sports, industry, counseling, and health psychology
Focus on positive human development, well-being, and personal growth
Limitations

Complexity of human behavior makes accurate measurement very difficult
Cultural differences mean motivation varies significantly across societies
Individual differences: the same behavior may arise from different motives in different people
No single universal theory completely explains all types of motivation
Conclusion
The concept of motivation has developed from philosophical ideas and instinct theories to modern
scientific approaches. Early psychologists emphasized instincts and drives, while later theories focused
on learning, cognition, emotions, and personal growth. Today, motivation is understood as a
multidimensional process influenced by biological, psychological, and social factors. Although no single
theory fully explains human behavior, modern motivational research has greatly improved the
understanding of learning, achievement, emotions, and personality in psychology.
